text: Do political parties truly listen to their voters? This article addresses this central democratic question by moving beyond traditional measures of positional congruence to examine whether parties align their agendas with voters' issue priorities. We argue that while parties generally reflect voter concerns in their election manifestos, this responsiveness is contingent upon party type. Specifically, large parties demonstrate a higher degree of attentiveness to public priorities, whereas governing parties tend to be less responsive to shifting voter demands. Furthermore, while niche parties are not more responsive across the board, they exhibit a heightened sensitivity to the concerns of their core supporters within their specific "owned" policy domains. To test these propositions, we integrate data from the Comparative Manifestos Project with voter priority metrics from the Comparative Study of Electoral Systems and various national election studies. Analyzing sixty-three elections across eighteen European democracies between 1972 and 2011, our findings offer significant insights into the mechanisms of political representation and the strength of democratic linkages.
